data.title: India Gold Market Update: Mixed Reading
data.description:
Highlights

  • Gold prices partially recovered in April1 following the sharp March decline, while import curbs and supply bottlenecks narrowed domestic discounts
  • Listed jewellers posted strong y/y growth in Q1 2026, driven by wedding as well as discretionary spends, higher ticket sizes, and ongoing expansion
  • Redemptions dampened gold ETF inflows, but holdings and investor participation continued to rise
  • Digital gold purchases remained firm, well above recent averages
  • Reserve Bank of India’s gold reserve holdings remained steady amid a decline in total forex reserves
  • Imports hit 9-month low in March.

Looking ahead

  • Demand is expected to be supported by the summer wedding season and key buying occasions such as Akshaya Tritiya2 and regional festivals, with price stability driving buying interest.

Partial price recovery following sharp March decline

Gold prices declined sharply in March in both US dollar and INR terms, marking the weakest monthly performance in nearly 13 and 12 years, respectively. Prices fell by 12% in USD and 8% in INR terms, with the relatively lower decline in domestic prices linked to the depreciation in the INR (by 4%). The price decline was driven by momentum factors – outflows from global gold ETFs and an unwind of COMEX net long positions, alongside higher opportunity costs due to a stronger US dollar and higher bond yields.

As of 13 April, prices had partially recovered and stabilised, although remained more than 13% below the January peak (Chart 1).3 The rebound has been supported by the persistent geopolitical risk premium, a softer US dollar and a pickup in inflows into global ETFs. On a y-t-d basis, gold prices remain elevated, up 8% at US$4,722/oz and 13% at INR 149,494/10g.4

Domestic gold prices, which had been trading at a sustained discount to international prices since mid-February, saw discounts widen further in March – from an average of US$15/oz to US$46/oz. Discounts have since narrowed significantly, averaging ~US$8/oz over the first two weeks of April.

The narrowing is attributed to tighter supply conditions following curbs on imports of platinum alloys (containing more than 1% gold by weight), along with broader restrictions on gold, silver, and platinum jewellery imports.5 In addition, anecdotal reports suggest operational bottlenecks, particularly delays in customs clearance of bullion consignments of nominated banks, further tightened supply.

Soft jewellery demand

Demand remained muted in the first half of March, impacted by seasonal and financial year-end factors as well as price volatility. Market feedback suggests that purchases were deferred during the price spike. However, as prices eased around regional festivals, demand improved but was overall subdued. Retailers focused on marketing campaigns and promotional offers to drive sales. Exchange of old gold jewellery remained a key contributor, reportedly accounting for 40–50% of sales, while investment demand for bars and coins continued to be robust.

Strong Q1 2026 for listed jewellers

Listed jewellery retailers6 delivered a strong performance in Q1 2026 (Jan–Mar), with revenue growth ranging from 32% to 124% y/y, making it the best quarter of the financial year for several retailers. Growth was driven by a robust wedding season and steady discretionary demand, which remained resilient despite the elevated and volatile gold prices. Regional festivals also supported sales. Retailers also benefited from an increase in the average ticket size of purchases, growth in plain gold jewellery, and a sharp rise in coin sales. Additionally, digital channels continued to gain traction, with some jewellers reporting over a fourfold y/y growth in online revenues.

Retailers continued their expansion push, adding between 7 to 38 stores during the quarter, although some launches were delayed due to geopolitical supply disruptions. Most retailers have indicated plans to continue expanding their store network, reflecting confidence in long-term demand and a focus to deepen market presence.

Overall, the quarter highlighted strong domestic demand resilience alongside strategic growth initiatives.

Gold ETFs: redemptions weigh on net inflows

Despite significant redemptions and in contrast with the trend of North American and European ETFs, Indian gold ETFs extended their inflow streak to the 11th consecutive month in March 2026, recording net inflows of INR 22.7bn (US$244mn) as per data from the Association of Mutual Funds of India (AMFI), above our initial estimate.7 This, however, was the lowest monthly net inflow in seven months (Chart 3). The moderation was primarily driven by record redemptions of INR 31.6bn (US$341mn), likely reflecting continued profit-taking, a trend carried forward from February. Nevertheless, cumulative gold holdings rose to 115t as of end March, in line with our initial estimates, indicating underlying resilient demand.

Following the relative weakness in March, April has seen a recovery in flows. During 1–10 April, net inflows stood at INR 17.36bn (US$187mn), contributing an additional 1.2t to collective holdings.

Q1 2026 recorded the strongest quarterly inflows into Indian gold ETFs, with net inflows of INR 316bn (US$3.45bn), resulting in an addition of approximately 20t to total holdings. Notably, nearly 80% of these inflows were concentrated in January, reflecting strong investor sentiments amid the rally in gold prices.

The investor interest in gold ETFs has been sustained and continues to broaden, albeit at a slower pace, as reflected in the number of accounts or folios of gold ETFs. March saw the addition of 0.31mn new accounts (vs 0.65mn in February), taking the total number to 12.39mn. Furthermore, gold ETFs’ share in the overall assets under management (AUM) of mutual funds has seen a rise to 2.3% from 0.9% a year ago.

Digital gold sees continued uptake

The purchases of digital gold via the Unified Payments Interface (UPI) remained strong in February, albeit below January’s record levels.8 Total purchases during the month totalled INR 30.3bn, equivalent to an estimated 1.9t (Chart 4) in volume terms, which is 53% higher than the average monthly volume of 1.2t over the preceding 13 months. Although digital gold purchases are currently unregulated, this channel continues to attract retail investors, likely driven by the ease of transactions and low minimum investment requirements. The value of these transactions has increased nearly fourfold over the past year, highlighting the growing need for regulatory oversight.

RBI holding onto gold reserves

Despite a 4% decline in RBI forex reserves to US$697bn over the five weeks to 3 April, its gold holdings have remained largely stable. The RBI added a marginal 0.17t to its gold holdings in the week ending 3 April, taking total gold reserves to 880.5t, accounting for 17% of overall forex reserves, up from 12% a year ago. Gold holdings have broadly remained steady since mid-2025, with the pace of purchases moderating in 2025 (4t) following significant additions in 2024 (73t).

Sharp decline in imports

Gold imports fell sharply in March 2026, declining 30% m/m and 59% y/y to US$3.1bn, a nine-month low. In volume terms, imports are estimated to be 20–25t, well below the 12-month average of 62t. The decline reflects a combination of demand softness and supply disruptions stemming from flight disruptions out of the Middle East, a key transit hub for bullion flows into India.

Footnotes

1 LBMA gold price PM and MCX spot gold price as of 13 April 2026.

2 Akshaya Tritiya- 19-20 April 2026.

3 LBMA gold price PM.

4 LBMA gold price PM and MCX spot gold price as of 13 April 2026.

5 Amendment in Import Policy of items covered under Chapter 71 of ITC (HS)2022, 02 April 2026

6 Titan Company, Senco Gold Ltd, Kalyan Jewellers India Ltd, PC Jeweller Ltd, PN Gadgil Jewellers Ltd.

7 AMFI reported March 2026 net inflows (~US$244mn) exceeds our US$177mn, estimated based on partial information.

8 Digital gold is a physical gold product that is purchased electronically by customers and held in professionally managed vaults until the customer chooses to sell the gold or take physical delivery. Providers of digital gold include payment applications, jewellers, and online investment platforms.

data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Trump threatens again to blow up all Iran's bridges and power plants

“Iran decided to fire bullets yesterday in the Strait of Hormuz — A Total Violation of our Ceasefire Agreement!” Trump posted, adding that his representatives would travel to Islamabad, Pakistan for negotiations.

Vance expected to lead Iran talks in Pakistan as major gaps persist

Vance is expected to be joined by envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner. Pakistan helped broker a two-week truce between Washington and Tehran that is set to expire Tuesday night. Iran has threatened to skip the talks, accusing the U.S. of making unrealistic demands.

Iran rejects second round of U.S. talks, cites ‘excessive’ American demands

The White House has publicly maintained that diplomacy remains possible, even as Trump has paired that message with renewed threats of military action if no agreement is reached.

Trump says U.S. Navy seized sanctioned Iranian-flagged ship in Gulf of Oman

“A US guided missile destroyer ‘intercepted the TOUSKA in the Gulf of Oman, and gave them fair warning to stop,” Trump wrote on a social media post Sunday. “The Iranian crew refused to listen, so our Navy ship stopped them right in their tracks by blowing a hole in the engineroom. Right now, US Marines have custody of the vessel.”

Oil surges, dollar gains, stock futures slip as Hormuz closure renews market anxiety

Markets reversed some of Friday’s optimism, when Iran’s brief reopening of the key shipping route had lifted stocks and pushed oil lower on hopes the seven-week conflict might ease.

data.title: Global Wealth Research - April 2026
data.description:
By Indrani De, CFA, PRM, Head of Global Investment Research FTSE Russell, David McNay, CFA, Director - Global Investment Research FTSE Russell, and Zhaoyi Yang, CFA, FRM, Sr Manager - Global Investment Research FTSE Russell

Middle East conflict injects volatility into markets. Bonds and “new defensives” when sentiment is weak, but markets returning to secular themes on de-escalation narratives: AI infrastructure, governance reform and EM.

The conflict in Iran injecting uncertainty and raising stagflation risk

  • Iran isn’t a significant oil exporter, only producing about 1.7mbpd, circa. 90% of which goes to China. However, the Strait of Hormuz is critical for global supply with circa 25% of all seabound oil (~20mbpd) passing the Strait, mostly destined for Asia. The most affected countries in Asia are South Korea and Japan, who import >85% of their energy (Source: IEA); China and India are also significant net importers of energy. China is better insulated than other Asian economies with an estimated reserve of 1.1 to 1.4 billion barrels (approx. 120 days).
  • The most direct vector for the conflict into markets is via higher energy prices, which affect inflation. 2Y inflation swaps in the US and Europe have increased by 1% and 1.3%, respectively, since the end of February. Markets are moving to price out further central bank rate cuts; Fed Funds Futures were pricing 2 further cuts by December 2026, both of which were priced out in March. Similarly, the 2 cuts expected by the Bank of England (BOE) have been repriced as 1-2 hikes; the BoE warned 1M households already facing higher borrowing costs because of the war in Iran.
  • At what level of oil price does demand destruction impact the economy? It is difficult to measure for a variety of factors – but we can simplify the question (at least in the US) by considering what level of gasoline price causes the US consumer to cut back? Work by the Dallas Fed after the 2022 shock would imply somewhere between $4.50-$5.00 per gallon – if this level holds true, then we may expect more weakness from the consumer. This may in turn increase political pressure on the US administration on finding a resolution to the conflict.
  • The probability of stagflation scenarios has increased. Increased energy costs will drive inflation higher, and supply constraints reduce productivity, which will pressure growth. Some countries, like the Philippines and Pakistan, have reduced working weeks in some sectors due to energy supply risks. This is notable because disruptions in countries which feed into low-margin goods in the global supply chain are a risk that could in turn lead to supply disruptions of mostly lower-value goods downstream.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of April 7, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD unless noted otherwise. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Volatility has increased, but what is it telling us?

a. Different measures all point to elevated risk going forward.

  • Prior to the conflict starting, equity volatility was relatively muted: the Russell Volatility Index (RVX) was below the mean, and vol-of-vol was low. Downside protection from options was inexpensive relative to average pricing of the 10Y average risk reversal.
  • Energy markets were faster to react. Option skew started moving higher in January – analogous to higher premiums for insuring against higher oil prices. Importantly, despite the sharp increase in the oil price in March, skew is still high to the call side. This means that even with oil at >$100/barrel, markets are paying large premiums to protect against another jump higher in oil prices.
  • RVX has trended higher, rather than spiking. Distinction being that unlike April ’25 when RVX spiked to >45 on US tariff announcements and then normalized quickly, during the Iranian conflict RVX has gradually drifted to less extreme but more stable volatility levels. This may potentially be a precursor to a more protracted period of elevated volatility, more akin to 2022.
  • The volatility of Russell 2000 skew had increased, but overall, the market remained sanguine during the first few weeks of the conflict. Only towards the end of March did skew push to the put side – implying markets are paying to protect against drawdowns.

b. Option skew primer

  • Covered in more detail in: What markets are telling us about the duration of the Middle East conflict
  • Option skew gives us an indication of how much the market is willing to pay for downside vs upside protection using options. The more negative the line, the more markets are willing to pay to protect their portfolios against a downside shock.
  • For this we look at the 3M Δ20 risk-reversal, which is the call implied volatility minus the put implied volatility; remembering a call gives the holder the right to buy at a given strike price and the put option provides the right to sell at a given strike.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of March 31, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Conflict of commodity prices: risk premiums are elevated even if there is de-escalation

a. Oil markets are pricing in material supply disruption over the next 6M

  • Oil curves, measured by Brent futures, were flat at the start of 2026, but since the start of the conflict, they have moved into a deep backwardation – reflecting the supply shock from a functional closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The steepness of the curve implies the market anticipates normalization of supply-demand balance over the next 6M. This assumption is a key risk for markets.
  • The level of the curve is circa. $20/barrel higher, meaning that the whole curve is more expensive than the start of 2026. This can be interpreted as an additional risk premium, which is important. If it persists at current levels, that implies ~1/3 increase in the price of oil year-on-year, which will have an impact on inflation expectations even if there is rapid de-escalation in the Middle East.
  • Longer-dated deliveries have been well anchored even as cease-fire talks commence, supporting the idea risk premiums will persist.
  • Whilst energy is the primary vector, the conflict is causing distortions in other commodity markets. Aluminum has rallied >10% after Iranian strikes on aluminum processing plants have impacted global supply. Longer-term demand for “energy transition” commodities (like lithium, nickel, and rare-earth minerals) may see further tailwinds due to accelerating demand for oil alternatives.

b. Was gold a hedge? It did its job

  • Gold fell 12% in March, the largest fall in gold in 35 event studies we looked at back to 1980.This raises the question about the role of gold as a risk and inflation hedge.
  • Higher oil prices have driven real yields higher, raising the opportunity cost of holding gold as a non-yielding asset.
  • Importantly, as a risk hedge, gold served its purpose. FTSE CRB Gold posted ~24% over 3M prior to the conflict – with a 52W equity-gold correlation of circa 0.0.
  • Gold typically acts as a store of value over time that is decorrelated to equity. That is distinct from an inflation hedge which is an asset that goes up when inflation rises.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of April 8, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Fixed Income yield has become more attractive since the geopolitical shock, with higher nominal and real yields

  • Nominal yields increased substantially in March in response to the Middle East conflict, contrary to the usual safe-haven dynamics, as markets focused on risks of higher inflation and hawkish-biased monetary policy. It was only from March 30 onward that yields declined, as safe-haven demand for government bonds re-emerged amid growing concern for longer-term economic growth. This implies a shift in market focus from inflation to growth. Yields staying elevated, inflation risk dominating safe-haven buying, sell-offs in both equities and bonds, all these point to stagflationary pressures.
  • As a result of sharper increases in short-term yields (largely driven by higher inflation expectation), 20s/2s curves bear flattened substantially in March (except Japan), led by Germany and the UK. Despite March flattening, 20s/2s curve steepness remained above long-term averages, with Japan at 230 bps and US & UK at 115 bps. This dynamic reflects secular forces (a recovery in term premium from inverted curves post 2022 rate hikes, meaning investors once again being compensated for holding longer duration) being confronted by opposing forces upon Middle East conflict shock (short yields rose more than longs, driving curves flatter).
  • 7-10yr yield increases in March were driven more by inflation expectation (vs real yields) in the UK and Germany than in the US, as Europe and the UK are more vulnerable to the energy supply shock than the US given their heavier reliance on energy imports.
  • Real yields on inflation-linked bonds (World ILSI) increased 25 bps in 7-10yr, and 32 bps in 20yr+, through March. Latest valuations of linkers look compelling on a historical basis, with real yields on both 20yr+ (2.6%) and 7-10yr (1.8%) at about the 98th percentile across 10-year history. Demand for near-term inflation protection drove 1-3yr real yields lower in March, reflecting higher market-implied inflation expectation. Breakeven inflation (BI) curves are currently inverted, with higher BI rate in 1-3yr, as markets had priced for a short-term disruption.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of March 31, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD unless noted otherwise. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

From Risk-on-Risk-off to Themes-on-Themes-off: RORO to TOTO

a. Markets moving between ‘defensive’ and thematic

  • Markets have been shifting between ‘defensive’ positioning and the themes that were driving markets pre-Middle East conflict; on de-escalation, EM, Korea, and Japan have rebounded.
  • The US has been the top-performing key equity market since the start of the conflict. The US status as an energy exporter and large tech weight helped relative performance.
  • Chinese equities were supported by a resilient RMB which underpinned returns in dollar terms. China’s strategic oil reserves and alternative supply (like Russia) provided a buffer. Lack of foreign ownership may also help explain China’s outperformance (China under 5% vs around 33% in Japan and Korea).

b. Markets reframing ‘defensive’ as Tech, Utilities & Listed Infrastructure

  • Returns from traditional defensive industries (Consumer Staples, Healthcare, Utilities, and Telecoms) have been mixed since the start of the conflict in the Middle East, highlighting how investors need to be more selective when protecting portfolios.
  • Energy has been the only industry with positive returns; although energy equity returns have been modest relative to the underlying commodity prices implying markets still aren’t seeing the crisis as a significant earnings tailwind.
  • Technology, particularly mega-cap names, has fared relatively well which reflects a flight to quality. Companies with strong balance sheets, global revenue streams and high liquidity were favored as being better insulated.
  • Utilities did well relatively but remain in negative territory since the start of the conflict. Utilities benefit from their traditional defensive nature with inelastic demand curves and limited margin pressure, as well as tailwinds from AI demand. The same dynamics supported FTSE Global Listed Infrastructure, which was only down -2.0% (to April 7, 2026); bolstered by regulated utilities and AI.
  • Consumer Staples, a traditional defensive, is suffering from higher input costs and expected pressure on margins.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of April 7, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD. * South Korea is classified as a Developed market in FTSE equity indices, account for 2.5% of market cap within FTSE Developed Index. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

Were traditional safe havens reliable during March?

  • Cross-asset performances and commodity returns saw even traditional safe havens like government bonds and gold decline during the March geopolitical conflict. Only the US dollar upheld its safe- haven role. In March, Commodities and listed Infrastructure (due to inflation-hedging characteristics) outperformed equities, in line with their historical outperformance in stagflationary environments. As a result of higher Commodities vs lower equities, Commodities relative performance vs equities rebounded strongly in March.
  • Commodities posted substantial returns of 19.5% in March, as crude oil price surged, alongside a rally in Aluminum price after attacks on production facilities. Oil price 1-month change of 63% ranks as one of the largest percentage moves in history. By contrast, precious and industrial metals including silver, gold, and copper, declined by 7.8-19.2% over 1M. This divergence within commodity sectors (higher oil vs weaker industrial metals such as copper, a growth indicator) may point to stagflationary risks.
  • Both equities and bonds saw sharp sell-offs in March, consistent with a stagflationary scenario, driving stock-bond correlation higher to 0.4, from -0.17 pre-conflict.
  • Like government bonds, gold did not perform as a reliable safe haven during the recent shock, down by 12% in March, likely due to its non-yielding nature and sensitivity to changes in real yields. Higher yields raise the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets such as gold. A stronger US dollar may also have weighed on global demand for gold, given its priced in dollars. Gold had already rallied strongly pre-Iran conflict (as had silver), so arguably it has served its purpose as a haven asset and now there is profit taking. Gold rebounded since Mar 31st as real yield eased.
  • The 2.4% appreciation of the US dollar in March (vs -4.1% over 12M) was supported by safe-haven inflows, the US’s position as a net energy exporter, and shifting expectations toward delayed rate cuts.

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. All data as of March 31, 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All returns are in USD unless noted otherwise. This report should not be considered “research” for the purposes of MIFID II. Please see the end for important legal disclosures. Results in this report are for research / illustrative purposes and do not represent the official performance of the indices.

data.title: Petrobras: Still Undervalued, But No Longer A Bargain As Iran Risks Cloud The Near-Term
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Petrobras (PBR) (PBR.A), I upgraded them to Strong Buy, backed by their record production, strong free cash flow, and continued expansion despite the fall in oil prices, supporting a solid long-term that can benefit from certain tailwinds.

With the stock returning about 62% since upgrading them to Strong Buy back in November, I'm downgrading PBR to a Buy, as the stock continues to offer a long-term opportunity, especially if we see a re-rating in the country's equities as interest rate trends advance alongside their major investment cycle finishing later in the decade.

Major Investment Cycle Advances

Petrobras reported a solid and overall expectable Q4 and 2025 as a whole, with the FCF dropping to $16.53 billion in 2025 compared to $23.32 billion in 2024, with elevated CAPEX as part of their plan ($19.52 billion in 2025 vs. $14.64 billion in 2024 and $12.11 billion in 2023), a vast majority (84%) of it going into E&P in 2025 and leading to their highest proven reserves of the past 10 years.

The company's renewed 5-year plan continues to assume elevated levels of CAPEX, although they would normalize and reduce drastically from 2029 and 2030 after remaining elevated for a few more years, as Petrobras aims at growing their asset base significantly.

Over the very long term, Petrobras will continue to play a crucial role for Brazil, as the company still aims at providing ~31% of Brazil's energy supply, which is expected to grow from 14 Exajoules in 2022 to 21 Exajoules in 2050, with low-carbon initiatives slowly added on top of the growth in oil and gas throughout this time.

Recently, PBR announced a change in leadership, with the country's former special secretary for legal affairs in the Office of the Chief of Staff to the President taking the interim Chairman role until the next general shareholders' meeting, further highlighting what state-owned and state-run companies mean and emphasizing the political risk that should be assumed when investing in a company like this, with PBR's CEO, for example, historically being approved by the country's President.

Note that this comes after the previous Chairman resigned back in March 2026 to take on a senior government role as Brazil's Minister of Planning and Budget, but let's not pretend that politics and major oil companies aren't intersecting in other countries as well from time to time - just look at Rex Tillerson, former Chairman and CEO of Exxon (XOM), who became the 69th U.S. Secretary of State a month after stepping down from there.

Financially, based on PBR's latest report, we continue to see a solid position, as even though the current assets are below their current liabilities, they still hold a very significant asset base that more than covers their debt, leaving us with ~$75.57 billion in equity (excluding non-controlling interests), with a weighted average outstanding maturity of 11.7 years as of the end of 2025 and a 6.7% average cost.

As a result of the environment and the drop in cash flow, PBR naturally reduced their dividend to a total of $8.11 billion in 2025 compared to $18.33 billion in 2024, emphasizing the volatility we should always keep in mind for a company like this.

Petrobras' next earnings report is scheduled for May 11, with expectations of an EPS of $0.91 and $27.24 billion in revenue. Although the Iran conflict can result in a near-term boost and help secure a higher dividend and the capital for a good chunk of the CAPEX, I don't expect major changes in the company's approach, which is something the CEO also highlighted during their Q4 Earnings Call:

I'd just like to remind you that we started last year with an oil price that was higher than $80, and we finished the year with less than $60, so that was $59. And the company delivered its results and showed that it has remained resilient and faced this price variation accordingly. At the beginning of the year, this volatility was again very high as a result of the war. But we still keep or stick to our internal policy, which remains solid. We looked at the oil and derivative pricing without transferring this volatility to the Brazilian domestic market. And this is something we've been doing several times.

Major Macro Developments

Something mentioned during the previous coverage is the end to the long-standing exemption on dividends in Brazil for nonresident investors following the Brazilian Federal Senate's move to approve a bill (that was later in the same month signed into law by the President) that now applies a 10% withholding tax on dividends from Brazil, effective January 1, 2026. I found this Q&A from EY published back in December that should be useful.

The Iran conflict has been key for PBR's performance in recent months, which comes alongside the ~10% YTD increase in the BRL against the USD. However, although Iran recently attacked a tanker about a day after the agreement due to the US' blockade on its ports, I still don't believe this should be seen as a positive for PBR over the medium- or long-term - even if the price will jump as a result.

At this point, there are two main scenarios that can stem from this. Either we see a quick end that's followed by war premiums lasting for quite some time given the significant damage already done by Iran's counterattacks in the region that can take months or even years to repair, or we see a recession, neither of which would be good for oil.

How the second scenario is bad for oil doesn't really need that much explaining given the effect it would have on oil prices, while the first can indeed result in elevated prices and inflated profits for some quarters, but once flows are back to normal, countries/companies are done replenishing reserves and won't rush in to buy any more, fundamentals can take over again, especially since the global economy is expected to slow down already because of the current war premiums.

Although PBR and peers can certainly use the current premium and at the very least pay another strong dividend or secure a good chunk of the financing needed for their long-term CAPEX program, it's unlikely to see elevated prices be sustained given the weak fundamentals we saw before, marked by high production and weakening demand, with the EIA pointing out the following during their recent market outlook:

We now expect that Brent crude oil prices will increase from an average of $81/b in 1Q26 to a peak of $115/b in 2Q26 before gradually falling to an average of $88/b in 4Q26. We base this on the assumption that the conflict does not persist past April and that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz gradually resumes but does not return to pre-conflict levels until late 2026. Shut-in oil production gradually returns as flows through the strait resume and oil trade flows adjust. Given this relatively long adjustment period after flows through the strait resume, we expect oil prices will remain elevated, with Brent crude oil prices averaging $76/b in 2027, about $23/b higher than in our February STEO forecast.

Sure, this is much better than previous estimates, and $76 is certainly amazing for such a low-cost producer like Petrobras (even $60 was good for them, but that's a PBR-specific advantage), but we'll see how the demand moves during this time, as there are significant uncertainties to keep in mind regarding how central banks will respond to the inflationary shock that's likely coming, since the region plays a vital role for global oil, LNG, fertilizers, and many by-products that can translate into higher prices for longer, with rate hikes already considered by the ECB shortly after the first strikes.

However, although PBR's previous expectations may seem obsolete now, they highlight that the biggest challenge for their five-year plan is a lower oil price environment, as their free cash flow breakeven was estimated at $59/bbl in 2026 as a result of the elevated CAPEX, although it's estimated to fall to $48/bbl.

Still, this can support moves such as their increased stakes in two Campos Basin assets for $450 million, funding their recent high-quality discovery there, or the planned acquisition of the Mataripe Refinery in Brazil (the second largest in the country) from Abu Dhabi's ‌Mubadala, further enhancing their portfolio with key assets.

Another major development since my previous coverage is the Brazilian Central Bank's decision to cut interest rates back on March 18, albeit by 25 basis points to 14.75% instead of the 50 b.p. the market hoped for, with the Iran conflict playing a key role in this despite inflation reportedly cooling down before it re-escalated.

Although further rate cuts are not guaranteed and it will ultimately depend on the situation in the Middle East, this remains a long-term positive, as even though PBR already has access to cheap debt through the country's BNDES, the market's attention can move once again from the country's high-yield notes into high-quality companies, as these cuts and improved growth expectations can benefit them.

This can potentially result in the market "adjusting" the dividend yield/returns it demands from these stocks through higher prices (a move from fixed income into equities), which is something we clearly saw in the US since the pandemic, with many REITs/other dividend stocks falling since treasury yields were more attractive and didn't justify the risk-reward. This is good, especially since companies like PBR are mandated by law to pay a dividend, as mentioned before.

Risks Reiterated

Although the Iran conflict offers a nice boost for PBR and it can result in a stronger dividend or improved financials, the aftermath once the initial shock passes can pressure oil prices and the company's valuation.

Also, even though there can be some long-term improvements from the country's rate cuts, the market will always account for the "Brazilian risk" especially given the fact that we're talking about a state-run company, with the recent change in dividend taxation certainly not helping.

Valuation

For PBR's DCF to Equity valuation, assuming no changes in net borrowings to highlight their pure cash generation potential without tapping into external capital, I'll use the same average oil price as in 2025, meaning WTI at ~$65.40 throughout the entire period, with a base FCF of $16.53 billion and $19.52 billion in CAPEX.

Based on their CAPEX expectations that should offset slight increases from production growth, I'm going to assume this FCF remains flat until 2028, after which it would jump to $21 billion in 2029 and $23 billion in 2030, followed by a 3% CAGR until 2035 while they'll reap the benefits of this major investment cycle, with a 2% terminal growth rate, a very elevated 15% discount rate to take into account the country-specific and commodity-related risks during a high CAPEX period, with 12.888 billion shares outstanding, meaning 6.444 billion given the 2:1 ADR conversion rate.

This way, we get to an equity value of $154.48 billion, for an intrinsic value of $23.97 per ADR, which is still above the current $20.45 despite using conservative estimates, highlighting that even now, the market is still approaching this very carefully.

Conclusion

PBR is rated Buy, as even though we can see more near-term pressure from the Iran conflict's inflationary shock, the stock remains undervalued, standing to benefit from a potential long-term re-rating.

There are certainly risks to keep in mind given the country and the industry, but the company remains crucial for the country's future, while long-term trends in their SELIC rate and the completion of their major investment cycle support a potential re-rating.

data.title: APi Group: A Safety Player At Work
data.description:
At the start of the year, I called APi Group (APG) an interesting safety player. Following strong momentum in 2025 as well as ambitious long-term targets, I concluded that I was glad that I had gotten to learn the business, as I was looking to update coverage in 2026, hoping for a better entry opportunity.

Since January, shares have risen nearly 10%, having outperformed the market at large by a comfortable degree, as the company announced a new and larger acquisition, now in Europe.

Other appealing serial value creators and M&A stores are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Adding Wtech Fire Group

Halfway April, APi announced that it had reached a definitive agreement to acquire Wtech Fire Group. Wtech provides fire protection, suppression, and detection solutions across Europe, with the deal seen closing in the second half of the year.

Based in Ireland, the company has activities across the continent, with total revenues seen around EUR 175 million, or just over $200 million in sales, with a margin profile that is consistent with APi's international business.

Unfortunately, no other financial details have been announced, so little can be said about the transaction or its attractiveness.

Making a Dent

Towards the end of February, APi Group reported a near 13% increase in 2025 sales to $7.91 billion, with organic growth seen near 8%. Momentum accelerated in the fourth quarter, when reported sales growth came in near a point higher, with organic growth rates reported just over 11%. APi reports its results across the larger safety services and smaller specialty services segments.

With Wtech announcing just over $200 million in sales, the deal is seen as contributing 2.5% to pro forma sales.

Adjusted EBITDA margins for the year rose by fifty basis points to 13.2% of sales, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $1.04 billion, after more pronounced margin expansion in the fourth quarter. All this drove a 20% increase in adjusted earnings to $1.48 per share. Momentum was strong, with fourth-quarter earnings of $0.44 per share, up ten cents on the year before, trending closer to $1.75 per share.

Net debt is reported at $1.85 billion, for a 1.8 times leverage ratio, although the company reports a net leverage ratio of just about 1.6 times. With 416 million shares now trading at a fresh high of $46 per share, the market value stands at $19.2 billion, for a $21 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at just over 2.5 times sales, just over 18 times EBITDA, and based on the annualized fourth-quarter results, shares trade at 26 times earnings here.

Of course Wtech will add to the reported growth. Ahead of the Wtech deal, 2026 sales were seen up to a midpoint of $8.5 billion, plus or minus a hundred million, with EBITDA seen around $1.17 billion, plus or minus thirty million. Of course, this guidance was provided ahead of the latest deal. This is seen reducing valuation multiples a bit, yet a mid-twenty times earnings multiple remains a best guess for the valuation going forward.

Adding to the 2028 Targets

APi Group is quite an interesting play, as it provides essential services and products surrounding fire, life safety, electronics security, elevators, and escalators. The company owns and operates strong brand names like Saval, APi, Viking, and Chubb, allowing it to grow in fragmented markets.

Actual products to think of include emergency and exit lights, hazard systems, fire suppression systems, fire alarms, pumps, sprinklers, maintenance, and service solutions, with specialty services including insulation, mechanical services, HVAC, scaffolding, and others.

A mere $1.5 billion business about a decade ago has seen massive growth to $8 billion here, with much growth driven by a transformative deal to acquire Chubb in 2022. Greater ambitions are seen, and based on the latest deal and outlook for 2026, a $10 billion revenue target for 2028 is very realistic, and perhaps conservative.

The company targets EBITDA margins around 16% of sales in two years' time, as that requires some real execution with margins up fifty basis points to 13.2% of sales in 2025, seen up to 13.7% in 2026. With two more years to go, a 15% margin seems about the best the company can do. That suggests $1.50 billion in EBITDA, about a 40% increase compared to the 2025 performance, creating a real roadmap for earnings to top $2 per share, perhaps as soon as 2027.

Upbeat, but Cautious

Since covering shares at the start of the year, shares have seen advancement. This makes it so that expectations have risen quite a bit here, to levels in the mid-twenties here.

The 2026 guidance looks solid, with continued growth seen across all metrics, while a bolt-on deal has been announced. While solid, it is difficult to see how good the latest deal is, as no financial details apart from a revenue contribution have been indicated. This is a larger bolt-on deal after the company announced no less than 14 bolt-on deals in 2025, with the balance sheet leaving much more financial room to continue its M&A streak here.

Continue to Watch

Following a strong 10% return in just three months, expectations have really moved up a bit. A mid-twenty times earnings multiple could be warranted given the strong performance, with organic growth hitting double digits in the fourth quarter, yet it still feels a bit rich amidst very manageable leverage.

The latest deal will add to the 2026 performance, and while I continue to cover shares with great interest, I am not yet convinced to get involved here, with shares trading at fresh all-time highs here.

data.title: Columbia Banking System: The Picture Has Changed (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have a very solid track record when it comes to investing. But even I misjudge things from time to time. A good example of this can be seen by looking at my rating of Columbia Banking System (COLB). Back in June of 2024, I made the decision to call the company a 'hold' candidate. However, I did claim that there was potential for the company and that an upgrade could be on the horizon. Since then, however, the stock has risen drastically, soaring 70.2%, while the S&P 500 is up 28.5%. This is clearly a missed opportunity. Looking at the data today, I see a firm that is actually doing quite well. Shares are attractively priced even after the stock has risen drastically. Asset quality is solid, and the growth trajectory of the business has been positive. In light of all of this, I would argue that upgrading it to a 'buy' is logical here.

Naturally, my opinion could change as the data warrants. And it just so happens that new data will soon be coming out about the company. On April 23rd, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue and profits are forecasted to rise nicely. This is great in and of itself. And it further supports my view that the stock is an appealing candidate if it does indeed come to pass.

Taking a Fresh Look at Columbia Banking System

With a market capitalization of $8.03 billion, Columbia Banking System is one of the larger publicly traded banks that I look at. For the most part, I focus more on smaller prospects, such as those with market capitalizations ranging between $200 million and $1 billion. But I do like looking at some of the larger players in the space. The fact of the matter is that, right now, Columbia Banking System is actually larger than it has ever been. This follows the successful completion of its acquisition of Pacific Premier in August of last year. This was not a small purchase, bringing in $14.5 billion worth of deposits. It also was responsible for the assumption of $11.4 billion of loans.

Speaking of the balance sheet, in the chart above, you can see how the balance sheet for the institution has changed in recent years. Thanks to the aforementioned acquisition, deposits shot up from $41.72 billion in 2024 to $54.21 billion last year. Interestingly, even though deposits increased, brokered deposits, which are high-cost deposits, fell from $3.03 billion to $2.36 billion. As you will see shortly, this has been part of the company's strategy to improve overall profitability. And honestly, it is working nicely. Perhaps my only concern with the institution is that an increasingly large portion of deposits are uninsured. I typically like for this to be no more than 30% of total deposits. But according to management, that number has risen from 34% in 2024 to 36% this year. That's not a deal breaker in and of itself. But it is undesirable.

During this same window of time, the value of loans jumped from $37.26 billion to $47.31 billion. The most significant exposure that the company has here would be the $10.28 billion tied up in multifamily properties. But there are other areas of concentration investors should be aware of. For instance, the firm has around $8.21 billion of non-owner-occupied term commercial real estate and $7.31 billion of owner-occupied term commercial real estate. It also boasts about $6.71 billion of term commercial debt and $5.62 billion of residential mortgages. In total, I don't see a lot to be worried about here.

Other aspects of the balance sheet have necessarily changed also. At the end of 2025, the company had securities amounting to $11.24 billion. That compares to the $8.43 billion that it had the year before that. Over that same window of time, cash and cash equivalents went from $1.88 billion to $2.38 billion. And debt rose only slightly from $3.78 billion to $3.84 billion. Considering how much larger the other parts of the balance sheet became, that's a rather small increase in debt if I do say so myself.

As the company has seen the size of its balance sheet increase, the size of its income statement has grown as well. From 2024 to 2025, net interest income jumped from $1.61 billion to $1.85 billion. Non-interest income grew from $211 million to $298 million. And net profits expanded from $534 million to $550 million. One contributor to this improvement ended up being its net interest margin, which grew from 3.57% to 3.83%. As the Federal Reserve slashed interest rates, the cost of interest-bearing deposits fell sharply from 2.87% to 2.36%. As the chart below illustrates, the company saw declining costs when it came to other liabilities. Yes, loans also yielded less during this time. But at least securities increased year over year. On the whole, however, the impact for the institution has been positive.

It would normally be tempting to value the company based on results from 2025. But considering the timing of the aforementioned acquisition that the business made, I would say that instead we should annualize results from the final quarter of the year, which would translate to forward net profits of about $860 million. In the chart below, you can see that this would mean a price-to-earnings multiple for the company of 9.3. That's actually below the 10 maximum that I typically like to see. Furthermore, it puts the company below all but one of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart. The subsequent chart below shows the priced book and price-to-tangible book multiples. The company is trading at just a slight premium to book value. And, on that basis, it's actually cheaper than any of the five banks that I stacked it up against. On a price-to-tangible book basis, meanwhile, only one of the five institutions ended up being cheaper than it is.

Asset quality should also not be ignored. Return on assets for the institution came in at 1.27%, according to management. The chart below shows that this puts the institution higher than any of the five banks that I compared it to. It's also above the 1% minimum that I typically like to see. The chart below shows that, on a return on equity basis, the institution is more in line with the competitors, with three of them actually being better off than it is. But even so, the 10.92% reading that we get here is above the 10% minimum I look for. I must also say that I am generally impressed with certain credit quality metrics of the company. In the final quarter of 2025, only 0.41% of its loans and 0.30% of its assets were classified as non-performing. These members are both lower than they were a year earlier, and they are below the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. So on the whole, credit quality is improving also.

It will be interesting to see what management has to say when they announce results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year on April 23rd. Thanks to the aforementioned acquisition that the institution made, analysts believe that revenue will climb from $491.4 million last year to $677.2 million this year. Considering the size of the acquisition and the improvements that we have seen in net interest margin, this is not inconceivable. Meanwhile, earnings per share are expected to grow from $0.41 to $0.64, which would mean a rise in net profits from $86.6 million to $189.2 million.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my assertion that, on the whole, Columbia Banking System is turning out to be a solid business. I honestly should have upgraded it long before now. Having said that, given the most recent financial performance that the company has achieved, I do think that it's not too late to upgrade it. That's why I've made the decision to bump it up from a 'hold' to a 'buy.'

data.title: MPLX: A Sound Growth Story Irrespective Of Iran Headlines
data.description:
Units of MPLX (MPLX) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 10% while also paying an 8% distribution. The past year was a transitional year for the partnership, with it investing significantly to expand operations, and we should see the benefit to cash flow this year. While the Iran War has created volatility in the sector, I see limited impact in the near term for MPLX and a net positive over the long term. I last covered MPLX in January, rating the stock a “buy,” and since then it has returned about 7%, exceeding the market’s 3% gain. Units had been up even more strongly, approaching my $60 price target before giving back some gain over the past three weeks. With a rapidly shifting macro environment for the oil complex, now is a good time to revisit MPLX.

As a logistics and transportation business, MPLX does not have direct exposure to commodity prices, instead earning a fee for transporting and storing oil and gas. As I will discuss below, I see the conflict potentially enhancing long-term growth in its gas operations, but the near-term impact will be quite muted. The same is also likely true of its oil and refined product business. One interesting point to track is that US oil inventories had been rising, and I expect this to reverse as there is likely to be a surge of US exports to replace some lost Middle East production. However, we are highly unlikely to experience any supply shortages (which could occur in some Asian markets if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for longer) given domestic production levels and current storage buffers.

Separately, I would note that as of year-end, Marathon Petroleum (MPC) owned 647 million units, or 64% of the MLP. These entities have a symbiotic relationship with MPLX’s oil infrastructure, closely linked to MPC’s refineries (95% of refined product transportation is for MPC). Importantly, Marathon is a strong entity, making it a sound counterparty; indeed, with wider crack spreads in the wake of the Iran conflict, its financial position is likely strengthening even further. Given MPC’s reliance on MPLX, I do not view it as likely to ever divest its controlling stake. At the same time, I see it as unlikely to ever try to reacquire the 36% it does not own and instead expect the status quo to persist for the foreseeable future.

In its most recent quarter, MPLX earned $1.17, which was $0.12 ahead of expectations as revenue rose by 6% to $3.25 billion. During the quarter, it generated $1.4 billion of distributable cash flow (“DCF”). EBITDA grew 2% from last year to $1.8 billion, but DCF was down 4% as it has taken on debt to finance growth projects. As these projects complete, cash flow should inflect higher, more than covering interest and accelerating growth over the next two years. I would add that for the full year, DCF grew by ~2% in 2025.

As you can see below, it has significant projects coming online this year and next, including significant ones like the BANGL expansion, Traverse and Eiger pipelines as well as LPG export terminals. This spending creates a clear line of sight for growth, especially as there clearly is the production to support these expansion efforts in its key plays.

Within its Crude Oil and Product Logistics unit, EBITDA grew by $37 million thanks to higher regulated rates, as the company can recapture all costs via higher tariffs. Its Natural Gas and NGL Services unit grew EBITDA by 2%, excluding the impact of a divestiture, with gathering volumes up 2%. Its Marcellus processing facilities are operating at 97% utilization, which is essentially as strong as it can get, speaking to the strength of production trends in the region. In fact, MPLX now transports 10% of all natural gas produced in the US.

During 2026, it plans to invest $2.7 billion on capex, including $2.4 billion on growth projects, up from $2 billion in 2025. While I do not see the Iran war creating more near-term cap-ex opportunities, more domestic production could support more cap-ex by MPLX for longer. 90% of its growth spending is focused on natural gas and NGL. While originally a crude & product-focused MLP, MPLX has increasingly shifted focus towards gas via both acquisition and organic spending to create a more diversified company. Over the medium term, I also see more growth in the gas than oil sector, given its use in electricity generation, especially with incremental demand from data centers. This spending is also focused more in the Permian, where production upside is greatest in my view.

One thing to note is that while the war in Iran has raised oil prices, it has not had any discernible impact on US natural gas prices. Unlike oil, which is a truly global market, natural gas is a local one, given limited international shipping capacity. With the US exporting all we can via LNG already, any disruptions in overseas flows do not really impact US natural gas prices. In fact, they are sitting near a 52-week low.

Now, over the longer term, I expect continued expansion in US LNG export capacity. If anything, the conflict in the Middle East strengthens my conviction in this view, as supply from the US is likely to be viewed as less risky. However, construction of export terminals, LNG “trains,” and associated infrastructure takes years to complete, costing billions. As such, this will not impact MPLX’s financials over the next couple of years, but it extends the runway for growth.

The low level of natural gas speaks to how abundant and low-cost production has become in the US, especially out of the Permian Basin in addition to the Marcellus, areas where MPLX has a leading position. While current levels of natural gas prices are not low enough to shut in production, they may limit drilling growth. Natural gas prices can be highly volatile, especially during periods of extreme weather (as it is the swing fuel for utilities). I view $2.50-$3.00 as the norm, and at $2.67, we are safely in that range. If prices fell closer to $2.00, that would create strain on E&Ps.

As you can see below, MPLX has built an integrated pipeline system across the Permian Basin in Texas. Its long-haul pipelines give landlocked products access to LNG export facilities where producers can sell closer to higher global prices. It has additional pipelines (those with dotted lines) currently planned and under construction to increase capacity to these export facilities. Again, given construction times, the demand from overseas for US gas will not provide additional support to 2026 results beyond the existing plan, but the runway for growth is long and getting even more robust.

It is important to note that MPLX has the financial strength to manage through this period of investment while also paying its 7.7% distribution. MPLX ended the year with a 3.7x leverage ratio, below the 4x I like to see in the pipeline sector. Given fee-based cash flows, pipeline operators can operate with a fair amount of leverage to support their capital program. Management has likewise confirmed it plans to stay at or below 4x. As you can see below, it steadily deleveraged to almost 3x in 2022-2024, and then leverage jumped back up in 2025.

That is because the partnership used its under-levered balance sheet in 2025 for M&A. It did $3.3 billion of net acquisitions in 2025, led by the $700 million purchase of the stake in the BANGL pipeline that it did not already own as well as a $2.4 billion deal for Permian assets. MPLX has taken on the debt from these projects but has yet to see a 12-month run-rate contribution from them in its financials. As these assets contribute to results and its own organic projects are complete, MPLX should naturally delever back closer to 3.5x by year-end. I remain comfortable with its balance sheet positioning.

At the same time, it wants to maintain distribution coverage of at least 1.3x. In 2025, distribution overage was 1.4x. MPLX also bought back $100 million of units in Q4, doing $400 million of repurchases in the full year. In the event that there is more opportunity for growth spending or incremental M&A, MPLX could always shut off buybacks, though that is not my base case. Ultimately, though, buybacks are a lower priority for management than the distribution and growth cap-ex.

MPLX’s commitment to its distribution has led to a successful track record, including 12 straight years of growth. Over the past five years, it has grown its distribution by 8% while also allocating more capital to growth projects and maintaining a healthy balance sheet. In 2025, the performance was even better with a 12.5% increase. While DCF growth was only ~2%, it used its excess coverage to support a similar 2026 increase, given the fact its 2025 investments should deliver incremental growth this year.

Back in January, I was targeting 7% distribution growth through 2030. I continue to expect this to be the case, and if we see further investments in US LNG export capacity, that growth runway could be even longer. Additionally, in an environment where headlines about Iran can cause dramatic moves in commodity prices and stocks, MPLX is a relative safe haven given its fee-based revenue streams and strong asset profile in key low-cost basins. I expect shares to rally back towards $60, and with a ~7.7% starting yield and 7+% distribution growth, I expect units to deliver a mid-teens return over the medium term. I remain a buy on MPLX.

data.title: Janus Living: Valuation Seems To Have Priced In Near-Term Upsides
data.description:
Investment Summary

I rate Janus Living (JAN) a hold because valuation seems to have priced in the upsides. But I do agree that the demand tailwind in senior housing is favorable, and JAN can take advantage of this tailwind via M&A easily given how fragmented the industry is.

Business Overview

JAN is an externally managed Real Estate Investment Trust [REIT] that owns only senior housing properties in the US. As of 31 December 2025, it owned 34 communities with 10,422 units across 10 states. All of its properties operate under a REIT Investment Diversification and Empowerment Act [RIDEA] structure. ~68% of units are in Life Plan communities, which are akin to continuing care retirement communities [CCRCs], where residents typically pay an upfront entrance fee as well as monthly charges. The rest are in traditional senior housing operating properties [SHOP], where residents generally pay regular monthly fees without a large upfront payment.

Industry Tailwind and Growth Outlook

Based on OECD and MS research, the US population aged 75 and older is expected to grow from ~26 million in 2024 to ~39 million in 2035. This comes at a time when new senior housing supply is very low. Specifically, units under construction in primary markets were only 19.6k at year-end 2025, or 1.3% of inventory, the lowest level since 2011. Simply put, demand is going to rise, but new supply is staying tight, and this should translate into higher occupancy over time. That said, one thing to note is that JAN's occupancy rate will not shoot up in a big way because Life Plan communities usually fill more slowly, as new residents often need to sell their homes and pay a large upfront entrance fee before moving in.

There is also another visible growth angle here. JAN could grow beyond its initial 34 properties, and it has capital to do that. After the recent IPO, JAN has raised $878 million of net proceeds, used $314 million to buy out a 46.5% JV interest in 19 properties, and completed another $360 million of acquisitions. If JAN can do this once, I don't see why it cannot repeat this M&A strategy since the industry is largely fragmented (source: S-1). The question is whether JAN has the financial dry powder to do this, which I think is yes. Post the IPO, JAN now has very low leverage with a $500 million revolving credit facility and a $100 million delayed-draw term loan facility.

Pure Play in Senior Housing

For investors that want pure exposure to this industry, JAN is probably the best bet, given that it only owns senior housing. First of all, it uses a full RIDEA structure, which matters because JAN does not just collect a fixed rent check from an operator. In a normal fixed-rent setup, the operator pays the landlord an agreed rent, and if the property performs better, the operator keeps most of that upside. Here, JAN is more directly tied to the actual operating performance of the properties. So, if occupancy rises, more units are filled and property-level cash flow improves. If pricing stays firm or margins get better, JAN benefits directly.

Secondly, the highlight is JAN's portfolio mix. ~68% of units are Life Plan, and ~70% of total units are independent living. This is a point to highlight because independent living residents usually need less care and fewer services, which makes the properties cheaper to run and can support better margins. They also tend to stay longer. The idea is that independent living residents are usually healthier and more self-sufficient, so they often do not need to move out as quickly for higher levels of care. This helps keep occupancy stable and enables a steady flow of revenue.

Third, I also think the Life Plan exposure is important. For context, Life Plan communities are senior living campuses where residents usually pay a large upfront entrance fee and then ongoing monthly charges. At first glance, JAN's heavy CCRC mix may look less attractive because it can seem less straightforward than a standard rental senior housing portfolio. But there is a positive side to this. JAN's assets are actually harder to copy because one would need more land, more capital, and to bypass more regulatory hurdles. In other words, this makes new supply harder to add and, in a way, limits competition. As such, my view is this Life Plan mix is not a weakness; it should be seen as a moat.

Valuation

Because JAN has just IPO-ed, we do not have a long trading history to benchmark against, but there are public peers to look at. For instance, Brookdale Senior Living (BKD) and Sonida Senior Living (SNDA). These peers trade at ~14x forward EBITDA, and I think that is a good start to think about JAN's valuation since they are all exposed to the same demographic and industry tailwinds. JAN's FY25 EBITDA is $165.2 million, and consensus is expecting this to go up to ~$392 million in FY28 and revenue to go from ~$693 million to ~$1 billion. This implies an incremental margin of 70%+, which does not seem impossible, as BKD management has said in their investor call that incremental margins can go to >80% when occupancy goes above 80%. If JAN does $392 million, at 14x, that implies an EV of ~$5.5 billion, or ~$6.3 billion market cap (after including the estimated IPO cash). That is not too far from the current market cap, and this is why, despite the clear fundamental story, I am giving this a hold.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for JAN because of valuation. The fundamental story is pretty straightforward. It operates in a favorable demand and supply backdrop, and on top of that, the balance sheet gives it flexibility to keep expanding through acquisitions and development. The only problem is valuation seems to have already priced in the near-term upsides.

data.title: AMD: $600 Bullseye
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) has soared to record highs after consolidation in the $200 to $260 range following the major chip deals with OpenAI and Meta Platforms (META). The reason for the recent breakout isn't clear, but AMD will greatly benefit from surging demand for CPUs, along with these massive AI chip deals. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock due to AI chip demand, with the CPU wave providing additional upside potential.

CPU Boost

While AMD has seen growing sales for AI GPUs, the company still relies heavily on CPUs for servers and PCs in order to generate the majority of revenues. In Q4'25, AMD produced record revenues of over $10 billion in the following segments:

  • Data Center - $5.4 billion
  • Client - $3.1 billion
  • Gaming - $843 million
  • Embedded - $950 million

The Data Center segment is nearly even between EPYC CPUs for servers and GPUs for AI at roughly $2.5 billion each. According to The Next Platform, AMD had AI GPU sales at $2.65 billion, leaving total Q4 non-GPU revenue at $7.65 billion.

All of this revenue is generally on the hook for higher chip prices due to soaring demand and limited supply growth due to AI GPUs absorbing all available capacity. Apparently, AMD hiked Ryzen CPU prices for PCs by 15% starting in April.

The Client segment was around $3.1 billion in Q4'25. AMD had total Client revenues last year in the $10 billion range, suggesting a 15% price increase leads to a $1.5+ billion increase in revenues over the next year.

GF Securities forecasts CPU server shipments to grow 28% YoY with revenue up 46% for 2026 from just below $9 billion last year. The analysts are forecasting upwards of $4.5 billion in additional EPYC CPU revenues, bringing the total CPU upside this year to nearly $6.0 billion alone.

The bullish case for AMD was always soaring GPU demand, but AI inference workloads are increasingly needing powerful CPUs to run correctly. Agentic AI requires orchestration and reasoning, requiring more CPU-intensive planning, playing right into the current sweet spot for the chip company.

AMD ended 2025 with nearly 29% unit share on CPU server chips with over 41% revenue share. The chip company grew revenue share by an impressive 4.9 points over Q4'24.

2027 Baseline Target

Bernstein just hiked 2027 targets to $76.7 billion with an EPS of $13.23. The revenue is nearly $10 billion above consensus due to what appears to be some analysts who haven't hiked 2027 numbers for the Meta deal for 6 GW of compute capacity just announced in February.

The analyst still only issues a $265 price target at just 20x the 2027 EPS forecast. The 2026 numbers seem extremely low with a base of $40.6 billion based on 2025 revenues at $34.6 billion and the potential for CPU sales to jump at least $6.0 billion.

The market is currently only forecasting $5 to $6 billion in sales growth in 2026 related to AI GPUs. The timing of the OpenAI and Meta deals ramping up in the 2H isn't fully known, but ultimately the market will value AMD based on 2027 numbers with those 2 deals full-speed ahead to start next year.

As with typical analyst estimates, the numbers are only as good as the recent updates. While the market has long forecast AMD to capture a solid portion of the $1 trillion AI chip opportunity, the company finally announced the deals to achieve these targets, plus the sudden surge in CPU demand has left the analyst community behind with estimates.

Analysts forecast 2027 revenue reaching $47 billion towards the end of last year. The OpenAI deal was announced last October, yet the numbers still don't include the $20+ billion in additional revenue from that deal, much less the additional $20+ billion from Meta and another $6+ billion from CPUs.

If anything, the Bernstein numbers still appear woefully low. The consensus estimates made last year were just above $47 billion, and AMD has deals worth upwards of another $46 billion, pushing the 2027 target to $90+ billion.

Ever since the OpenAI deal was announced, with the last tranche of shares requiring a stock price of $600, AMD has had this bullseye toward this price level. For now, though, investors can take the Bernstein $13.23 EPS target for 2027, and a simple 20x multiple offers a simple baseline value of $265.

AMD has already promoted an EPS target topping $20. The stock reaching the $600 goal seems very doable with surging CPU demand and the additional Meta deal.

Elevated Risks

Naturally, the risks are two-fold: AI demand doesn't meet forecasts, or competitors create better chips. On the latter, Cerebras is rumored to have signed a $20 billion chip deal with OpenAI.

AMD has these mega chip deals lined up, but the company could run into execution issues with each deal requiring more GPUs than the company sold in 2025. The stock trades at 42x 2026 EPS targets, with estimates mostly in line with where the business is now before the big AI ramp. Any AI chip issue, and AMD would easily fall back below the $180 level where the stock traded before the OpenAI deal announcement.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that AMD remains incredibly cheap due to the market not fully factoring in the major upside from the massive AI chip deals, plus the addition of higher CPU demand and prices. The stock only trades at 20x realistic 2027 EPS targets, while the growth rates will vastly exceed this valuation multiple, and the ultimate earnings potential supports a bullseye on the $600 target.

data.title: S&P 500: I Sold Too Early, What Now? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Last week was, to put it mildly, eventful. When the S&P 500 (SPY) futures sold off to 6730 on Sunday following the breakdown of US-Iran negotiations, not many would have expected it to close the week at 7126. I certainly didn't, and after buying 6740 in line with last weekend's article, I closed way too early when prices exceeded the previous week's highs.

So what now? Chasing the rally at 7126 doesn't seem to be a wise move, but on the other hand, there have been no decent dips to buy. Indeed, since the 6316 bottom, there has been only one red session, and that closed only 0.11% lower.

This week's article looks at why this rally should continue and ways to participate without taking too many risks.

S&P 500 Monthly

April tends to be a bullish month, and it is already over 9% higher and trading at new highs. How high can this move go?

That depends on whether the move from 6316 will be a whole new phase of trend, similar in size to previous ones of over 2000 points, or whether the move is still finishing off the rally that started at the April 2025 lows. If it is the former, equality with the 2025-2026 rally would target 8484. That's not impossible, but it seems a stretch, so I am leaning toward the latter option. That would lead to lower targets, and the only major one in the area is 7490. That is where the 2025-2026 rally would be equal to the 2022-2025 rally.

Supports on the monthly chart are way down at 6747 and 6316.

A DeMARK exhaustion count was completed in February and had a brief effect with the March drop. No new signal is due this year.

S&P 500 Weekly

This week's move was even larger than the rally during the week of the ceasefire, with a gain of 4.54%. We found out why on Friday as the Strait of Hormuz was finally reopened.

The weekly chart is clearly bullish, and the close at the highs projects follow-through early next week. That said, with a 13% gain in just 3 weeks, there may be a pause, especially since most of the possible good news is now out. Many 3-bar rallies pause on the 4th bar.

One observation is that the patterns in 2026 - and to a lesser degree, the drivers (geopolitics/Trump policies) - have been similar to 2025, just smaller.

The tariff crash recovered strongly and rallied to the 161.8* extension of the drop at 6958 (regular readers will remember that was my target for the rally).

Given the similarities, the current rally could also reach the 161.8* extension. Interestingly, that comes in at 7424, not too far off the 7490 target on the monthly chart.

The previous high of 7002 should now act as support. Below there, the high-volume area and the 20SMA at 6850ish is the next area of interest.

The DeMARK exhaustion count from the week of the low has had a significant effect. No new counts can be completed for another 7 weeks.

S&P 500 Daily

Most of my trades are based around buying dips against support levels. That was a challenge last week, as each session closed higher, and the largest dip was only 32 points. It was unusually strong, and moves of such magnitude tend to come after sustained bear markets, not so close to all-time highs after a drop of only 9.67%. That supports the view that this is a blow-off top rather than the start of a sustained new trend.

The daily chart shows that Friday's good news rally held onto its gains, and that keeps the immediate bullish bias.

7002 is obvious support at the previous highs but may not be reached. 7040-51 is the first potential support.

A DeMARK exhaustion signal completed on Tuesday and had no effect whatsoever. That can happen in strong uptrends, but the second signal often works. In any case, no signal is due to be complete next week.

Drivers/Events

The Israel-Lebanon ceasefire on Thursday facilitated Friday's reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. This was the best possible news for markets, and oil dropped sharply. I think it will eventually return to the $60s, but it could take time, and as I am writing this article, I just read Iran has closed the Strait again in response to the US blockade. Also, a tanker has reported being attacked. I don't know what will happen between now and the Sunday reopen, but this could lead to another Sunday futures wobble like last week.

A peace deal would be the icing on the cake, but a date for a second round of negotiations has not been set. While progress is being made, there are still some contentious issues, and there could be several weeks more of negotiations and headlines. That could actually support the rally as the good news is teased out rather than delivered in one conclusive announcement. If and when that happens, it may be a case of "sell the news." For now, we are still "buying the rumor."

Next week's scheduled events and data releases are again on the light side. Fed Chair Designate Kevin Warsh is due to give a testimony to the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs on Tuesday, which may give stocks a boost.

Probable Moves in the S&P 500

While I can't completely rule out another rally of over 2000 points like the last 3 major uptrends, this year's drop of 9.76% does not look large enough to have set up a move of that magnitude. A more likely target is the 7424-7490 area. That could lead to a 20% drop later this year, and I still think the S&P 500 "needs" a larger drop before it can trend sustainably.

The near-term issue is where to buy for the target in the 7400s and what would make that target invalid (or less likely). Last week's re-open dipped around 120 points into the Sunday futures low, and a comparable drop this week would target 7027ish. 7002 is the obvious major level at the previous high but may not be reached.

Dropping through 7002 would not be ideal, but it wouldn't be a reason to immediately shift bearish. I'd get more concerned with a drop below 6900, as that would likely lead to a test of 6740-64 and have implications going forward.
